benchmarks.title: Behavioral Metacognitive Control on Expert Questions in LLMs

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is unambiguous: GPQA Diamond correct-answer letters plus empirical per-question difficulty drive deterministic scoring rubrics (delegate-game reward table, routing-alignment thresholds, entropy calibration, type-2 SDT). Robust backward-iterating JSON parsing with keyword fallbacks handles malformed output.

2_construct_validity.justification: Every notebook sends real prompts to the injected llm and scores the model's actual responses (e.g. delegate_game scores ANSWER vs DELEGATE choices; t11 computes MC=d*/d-hat from binary judgments). Design isolates behavioral vs declared metacognition with paired neutral/affective framing (t06) and difficulty-keyed delegation, deliberately minimizing confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular code with _safe_prompt error handling, dedicated parsers, per-question CSV logging, graceful partial-completion on API failure, and a no-scipy norm_ppf implementation. Well-documented headers per task.

4_sample_size.justification: Each task iterates 80 GPQA Diamond questions (t11 runs 160 prompts as 2-per-question pairs), well above the 20-item Level-2 threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes multiple distinct dimensions: t01 reports mean reward, delegate rate and parse failures; t02 combines parseability, differentiation and routing alignment; t04 measures entropy calibration error; t11 computes hit/FA rate, d-hat, type-2 AUC and MC efficiency. Calibration vs accuracy are explicitly separated and justified in markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook headers articulate the exact capability (behavioral vs declarative metacognition), why it matters (acting on uncertainty), and the gap relative to prior work, citing CIKM/TheWebConf/GPQA references and the MSV framework.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific sub-capabilities with explicit hypotheses and experimental controls: difficulty-graded delegation, paired neutral/affective conditions (t06), signal-present/signal-absent SDT trials (t11), and behavioral-vs-declared coherence comparison.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are stratified by empirical difficulty and category, with results broken down by difficulty thresholds (easy <0.45 vs hard >=0.45 routing scoring) and category fields preserved in output CSVs, enabling a performance gradient.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is fully defined in code: GPQA correct_answer letters, empirical difficulty values, and explicit multi-tier scoring rubrics (e.g. ANSWER correct+conf>=3 -> 1.0; DELEGATE w/ difficulty bands; routing-alignment table; SDT-derived MC). benchmarks.title: Russian Doll Bench: Infrastructure Building Capabilities

1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is a deterministic terminal status (online/total subsystems brought ONLINE); each terminal (e.g. Sys32Terminal) validates exact auth tokens via reversible codecs, so ground truth is unambiguous and machine-verifiable in code.

2_construct_validity.justification: run_agent_loop genuinely sends prompts to the model with file/run tools and scores the model's actions against the live terminal server; nested-terminal generators of increasing complexity isolate agentic problem-solving and cross-run learning, matching the stated purpose.

3_code_quality.justification: infrastructure.py/tasks.py are modular and well-commented with robust handling: separate GenAI vs OpenAI tool loops, HTTP retries, malformed-content guards, checkpoint restore, and graceful RunResult fallbacks on exceptions.

4_sample_size.justification: run_adaptive_learning defines 10 generators of escalating nesting, each yielding multiple terminal configurations validated per iteration — far more than 20 evaluation items across the difficulty curve.

5_metric_design.justification: Score combines two distinct dimensions — number of fully-passed generators (capability) minus an efficiency 'fine' based on iterations/tokens spent — and the protocol is documented in system.md.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task name/description name the capability ('cross-run learning and progress retention'), and system.md states the goal, but the notebook's task-page markdown is sparse and offers little positioning against existing evaluations.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Source code is heavily commented and the dataset/import setup is explained, but the notebook itself has only minimal markdown (install/import/run) with no step-by-step methodology walkthrough on the task page.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (mastering activation of arbitrary nested-terminal configs and retaining knowledge across runs) with an explicit difficulty gradient and efficiency hypothesis baked into the generators.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized into 10 difficulty tiers built from distinct terminal types (Sys32/BitMixer/Cipher/Hash/Maze) with increasing nesting, and the harness reports where the agent first fails.

10_model_coverage.justification: No saved run files or model identifiers are observable in the provided materials; only a single model run is configured, so multi-model coverage cannot be confirmed.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is verified deterministically by the terminal server's status endpoint (online/total terminal counts); a terminal flips _online=True only when the model sends the correct decoded/keyed payload (e.g. MazeTerminal._handle_activate checks decoded == activation_phrase). Ground truth is fully computable and unambiguous.

2_construct_validity.justification: Genuine agentic eval: run_agent_loop sends a system prompt and tools to the model, executes the model's tool calls against a live nested-terminal server, and scores how many terminals the model brings online. The claim (cross-run learning / mastering unknown nested terminals) is tightly operationalized; score is structurally driven by model actions, not internal data.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular package: @kbench.task decorators, separate envs/infrastructure/telemetry/terminal modules, robust tool dispatch with arg validation, retries on status, graceful RunResult error handling, and dual GenAI/OpenAI loops handling empty-message edge cases.

4_sample_size.justification: run_adaptive_learning defines 10 progressively harder generators (T1 through nested T1|T2|T3|T4|T5 composites), each yielding multiple terminal tasks validated per iteration; the effective evaluation surface (validation terminals across generators) is well over 20.

5_metric_design.justification: Beyond pass/fail online-count, the harness reports multiple distinct dimensions: terminal_activation_steps, iterations spent, token metrics, and a composite score that fines for iterations spent (efficiency) — capturing both capability and token/iteration efficiency.

6_problem_statement.justification: The system.md prompt and task description clearly state the targeted capability (cross-run learning, progress retention, mastering nested-terminal activation efficiently), but the captured notebook markdown is thin and does not situate the task against existing benchmarks or a broader gap.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Source package is extensively documented: per-terminal .md specs (role/commands/rules), docstrings on infrastructure and scoring, prompt files, and unit/trace tests; the notebook explains dataset import and environment setup. Methodology and scoring are reproducible from code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability (long-horizon agentic reasoning + cross-run knowledge persistence) with an explicit difficulty gradient (single -> deeply nested heterogeneous terminals) and persistent filesystem mechanism, allowing attribution of performance to the claimed skill.

9_item_diversity.justification: Five distinct terminal types (Sys32, BitMixer, Cipher, Hash, Maze) combined via a structured generator ladder of increasing nesting depth and type-mixing, with per-terminal activation tracking — a clear taxonomy enabling a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Notebook runs a single kbench.llm per execution; only one model run is evidenced (claude-opus artifact), no cross-model comparison present in the captured material.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is deterministic and machine-verifiable: each terminal puzzle (Sys32/BitMixer/Cipher/Hash/Maze) has a server-side correctness check (status['done']/online/total) plus per-terminal unit_tests.py and trace_test.py. The agent's success is judged by whether it actually solves the terminal, not by string matching.

2_construct_validity.justification: Headline eval (run_adaptive_learning -> adaptive_learning) runs a genuine agentic tool loop: run_agent_loop calls the model via _run_genai_tool_loop/_run_openai_compatible_manual_tool_loop against a live terminal server, scoring cross-run learning by how many progressively harder generators it solves. The progressive T1..T5 generator ladder isolates 'adaptive learning / progress retention' specifically.

3_code_quality.justification: Substantial, modular infrastructure (server/, terminals/, VFS, telemetry, checkpoint save/restore, free-port server, deterministic seeding rnd=Random(42)) with per-terminal unit tests. The notebook's import error (kaggle_benchmarks.envs.LocalEnvironment missing) is a version-pin/environmental artifact, not a construction defect; the imported package logic is clean and well-organized.

4_sample_size.justification: The eval set is the 10 task generators (T1, T1-T1, ... composite). As distinct evaluation items this is well under 20; the benchmark is a single adaptive ladder rather than a large item bank, so sample size for statistical comparison is low (1-9 effective tiers).

5_metric_design.justification: The score combines number of fully-passed generators with an iterations-spent 'fine' (score = 100*passed - min(99, iters/passed)), blending capability and efficiency into one scalar. Telemetry tracks iterations, but the reported metric is a single composite leaderboard number with no second independent dimension reported.

6_problem_statement.justification: The @task description states the target ('capacity for cross-run learning and progress retention by presenting increasingly complex versions of a task'), naming the capability and method, but the notebook markdown is thin and does not situate the gap relative to existing agentic benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The dataset package is the documentation: docstrings, per-terminal .md specs, and structured code. But the notebook itself has only brief markdown (install/import/run), and there is no consolidated description of scoring rules/schema for a reader, requiring code reading.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (incremental skill retention under escalating difficulty) with a built-in difficulty gradient (T1 single terminal -> multi-terminal composites) acting as an experimental control; success at higher tiers attributable to learning/transfer rather than one-shot luck.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are an explicit difficulty taxonomy: a 10-step generator ladder mixing terminal types (Sys32/BitMixer/Cipher/Hash/Maze) and composition depth, with the harness reporting which generator/task the model failed on (_find_easiest_unsolved_task emits failed_on_generator/failed_on_task), enabling a where-it-fails gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: No run.json artifact or confirmed model run is present (the notebook errored at import due to a version-pin mismatch, an environmental issue). No evidence of more than one model; deterministic count is 1.

benchmarks.title: Can LLMs Inhibit Optimization in Supply-Chain Decision-Making?

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is fully deterministic: compute_delta_nll() scores the LLM's per-round orders against a Sterman-calibrated human-session simulator using a Gaussian NLL, with explicit warmup handling, leave-one-human-out (LOHO) baseline, and assertions that each role produces N_ROUNDS non-negative orders. The metric is a well-specified numerical quantity, not subjective.

2_construct_validity.justification: The primary eval genuinely measures a model: run_beer_game_with_llm() sends per-role prompts via llm.prompt() each of 24 rounds and parses the LLM's order quantity, then scores those real orders (ΔNLL). It targets supply-chain executive control/bullwhip suppression and uses a deliberate baseline-vs-enhanced-prompt pair (task-1 vs task-2). Confound: scoring is 'human-likeness' (proximity to Sterman human heuristic), so a model that orders optimally rather than human-like is penalized, partially conflating the claimed 'inhibit optimization' construct with mimicking human behavior; parse_order_from_response grabs the first integer which can misread free-text, adding mild output-parsing confound.

3_code_quality.justification: Code is functional, modular (Player class, eta-based pipelines, fresh kbench.chats.new() per role/round) and runs end-to-end. But it is essentially pure code with zero markdown documentation, parse_order_from_response has a fragile first-integer regex with only a silent 0 fallback, and there is no validation of malformed LLM replies beyond defaulting to 0.

4_sample_size.justification: NUM_SESSIONS=11 sessions x 4 roles = 44 ΔNLL evaluation units per run, well above 20, each session being a full 24-round game; ample for aggregate comparison.

5_metric_design.justification: A single performance dimension is computed: mean ΔNLL/step (LOHO-NLL minus LLM-NLL) aggregated across roles/sessions. It is a thoughtfully chosen, non-trivial metric (relative to a leave-one-out human baseline) but only one dimension; no calibration/latency/cost or second axis, and the justification lives only in code comments/docstrings, not markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: The task title ('Can LLMs Inhibit Optimization in Supply...') and code docstrings name the capability (baseline executive control / bullwhip in the Beer Distribution Game) and the ΔNLL-vs-human framing, but there is no task-page markdown articulating why this matters or what gap it fills relative to existing evals.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The Sterman 11-session parameters, simulator, and ΔNLL scoring are spelled out in code with section-banner comments and docstrings, so a reader can reconstruct the protocol, but there are no markdown cells documenting dataset provenance, schema, or a step-by-step walkthrough; documentation is partial and code-only.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability (suppressing the prepotent over-ordering/bullwhip response under delayed feedback) with an explicit experimental control: two notebooks form a paired baseline prompt vs. enhanced decision-guidelines prompt, allowing attribution of behavioral change to the prompt manipulation, and per-role ΔNLL isolates where breakdown occurs.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are structured along two labeled axes reported separately: 11 distinct Sterman human sessions (each with different calibrated parameters) crossed with 4 supply-chain roles (retailer/wholesaler/distributor/factory), and the code prints per-role ΔNLL each session, enabling a performance gradient across roles and sessions.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook ends with a single beer_game_baseline.run(kbench.llm) invocation against one model; there is no evidence of running multiple distinct models.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is the LLM's per-step order quantities scored deterministically via ΔNLL against Sterman's 11 calibrated human sessions and a leave-one-human-out baseline (compute_delta_nll, nll_gaussian). Scoring is fully reproducible numerical logic with warmup handling and N-clamping; no ambiguity.

2_construct_validity.justification: The headline eval genuinely calls the model (llm.prompt) for all 4 roles over 24 rounds and scores its actual orders. The folder ships paired conditions (nb_task-1 baseline prompt vs the enhanced 'decision guidelines' prompt) that manipulate the same mechanics, isolating whether the model can inhibit prepotent over-ordering (the bullwhip effect) versus instruction scaffolding.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular single-cell implementation: separate Player class with eta-based pipelines, a Sterman simulator, ΔNLL functions, robust parse_order_from_response with line-skipping fallback and a 0 default, and kbench @task/assertions. Lacks markdown but code itself is well-structured and commented.

4_sample_size.justification: NUM_SESSIONS=11, each producing 4 role time-series scored independently (44 ΔNLL units per task variant) over 24 rounds; far exceeds 20 evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single performance dimension is reported (mean ΔNLL/step across sessions and roles). It is a sophisticated calibrated metric, but no second distinct dimension (e.g. cost, calibration) is computed, and justification is via docstring only.

6_problem_statement.justification: Only the in-notebook docstring/title ('Baseline Executive Control — Beer Distribution Game') names the capability with minimal motivation; no broader research framing or gap statement is available as a task page.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Code is self-explanatory with section banners, but there are no markdown cells walking through dataset provenance (the embedded Sterman parameters), the ΔNLL scoring rationale, or metric interpretation; reproduction requires reading code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (inhibiting over-optimization / bullwhip amplification) with the baseline-vs-enhanced-prompt pair acting as an experimental control, and an explicit hypothesis that more human-like (less amplified) ordering scores higher ΔNLL.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary by the 11 distinct human sessions and 4 supply-chain roles, and ΔNLL is reported per role and session, giving some breakdown; but there is no explicit difficulty taxonomy or labeled sub-condition tiering.

10_model_coverage.justification: The task is wired to the single kbench.llm abstraction; no model identifiers appear and only one model is run.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Scoring is a deterministic continuous metric (mean ΔNLL/step comparing LLM orders vs Sterman human-calibrated baseline vs leave-one-human-out baseline) computed in nll_gaussian/compute_delta_nll. There is no exact ground-truth answer per item; the 'better-than-LOHO' baseline is reasonable but the metric's sign/scale is not anchored to clear pass/fail thresholds, and parse_order_from_response regex-extracts the first integer with a brittle WAIT-of-default fallback, so verification of the model's intended order is fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: The headline eval genuinely sends per-round prompts to a model (kbench.chats.new + llm.prompt inside run_beer_game_with_llm) and scores the model's actual order decisions against human-likeness, so it measures a model. However, it claims to probe 'executive control / inhibiting optimization' yet the metric only measures how closely the LLM mimics human Sterman ordering behavior (ΔNLL vs human baseline); higher human-likeness is conflated with the claimed inhibitory-control capability, and order-parsing/format compliance is a visible confound, giving partial but imperfect alignment.

3_code_quality.justification: Code is well-organized into clearly sectioned modules (simulator, scoring, Player class, prompt builder, runner, kbench task), uses @kbench.task and evaluate-style structure, fresh chat context per role/week via kbench.chats.new, and includes a robust eta-based pipeline simulation; parsing has a fallback. Two near-duplicate notebook variants (baseline vs enhanced-prompt) are clean and idiomatic.

4_sample_size.justification: NUM_SESSIONS=11 sessions, each producing ΔNLL across 4 roles = 44 scored data points per task, with 24-round trajectories each; well above the >20 threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: A single principled performance dimension is computed (ΔNLL/step human-likeness) with markdown/docstring justification, plus assertion checks for order count and non-negativity. It does not compute a second distinct dimension (e.g., cost efficiency vs human-likeness, or bullwhip/variance amplification), so it stays at one underlying dimension.

6_problem_statement.justification: The notebook docstrings name the capability (baseline executive control in the Beer Distribution Game, inhibiting over-optimization) and describe the ΔNLL-vs-human design, but there is no separate task-page markdown connecting it to a broader research gap or explaining why this beats existing supply-chain LLM evals.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Extensive in-code section headers and docstrings document the simulator, Sterman parameters, scoring formula, and runner. However dataset provenance for _STERMAN_11 (the 11 calibrated human sessions) is only asserted, not cited, and there are no markdown narrative cells explaining metric interpretation/edge cases for a reader, leaving partial documentation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a named domain (supply-chain decision-making / inhibitory control via the Beer Game) and includes two conditions (baseline prompt vs enhanced decision-guidelines prompt) that act as a manipulation. But there is no congruent/incongruent control isolating inhibition specifically; performance reflects general human-mimicry plus prompt-following, so a specific sub-capability is not cleanly isolated.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary naturally across 11 distinct human-parameter sessions and 4 supply-chain roles, and results are printed per role and per session, giving some natural variation. But there is no structured difficulty taxonomy or labeled sub-condition stratification (e.g., demand-shock severity tiers) reported as a designed gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook runs a single injected kbench.llm via .run(kbench.llm); no model identifiers and no multiple-model comparison appear in outputs or metadata, so coverage is effectively one model.

benchmarks.title: The Time Traveler's Dilemma: Empathy, Oversight, & the Stop Sign Effect

1_answer_correctness.justification: Deterministic word-count and lexical-guillotine checks are exact, but the semantic dimensions rely on a 3-4 criterion judge panel that is pass/fail with no anchored score scales or examples per level, leaving open-ended judgments fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: Notebooks genuinely prompt the target model, but the claimed metacognition/empathy is largely operationalized as instruction-following, lexical-constraint adherence and metaphor mapping; the cross-language vs English baseline pairing gives some signal but confounds remain.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional with regex extraction and a multi-judge loop, but rough: each notebook is a single monolithic function, judge failures are silently skipped, and there is no fallback for missing bracket encapsulation beyond an immediate fail.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook's evaluation is a single prompt/item (one historical-figure scenario), far below the 10-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple distinct checks (word-count, lexical purity, semantic judge panel) are combined and a 4-judge panel mitigates single-judge bias, but they aggregate to pass/fail with minimal justification and no calibration/latency dimension.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task descriptions name the capability (cross-temporal/epistemic-exile explanation) clearly, but provide limited motivation or gap analysis versus existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebooks include docstrings and inline section comments, but lack task-page markdown, dataset schema, or documented scoring edge cases.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the right domain (constrained cross-era explanation) and uses paired language/English-baseline conditions, but does not isolate a single mechanism within one task.

9_item_diversity.justification: Within any one notebook items are homogeneous (single scenario); across notebooks there is variation in era/language/topic, but no within-task taxonomy or difficulty stratification reported in code.

10_model_coverage.justification: The four model keys present are the hardcoded judge 'senate'; the target model is a single kbench.llm per run with no evidence of multiple target-model runs.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Deterministic guards (word-count window, regex lexical bans, bracket-block extraction) are unambiguous, but the qualitative core uses a 4-criterion LLM-judge panel whose criteria are yes/no questions without anchored per-level examples, so the rubric covers ~4 dimensions but vaguely.

2_construct_validity.justification: Headline eval genuinely calls the target model (llm.prompt) and scores its actual response via deterministic + judge checks. However the task claims 'empathy / epistemic exile / AGI metacognition' while most of the signal is constraint-following (300-400 word count, forbidden-token avoidance, bracket formatting) — a moderate confound conflating the claimed capability with instruction-following.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional single-cell tasks with reasonable regex extraction and a senate-integrity guard, but minimal structure: a hard FATAL assert if exactly 4 judges aren't present, no normalization fallbacks, and substantial copy-paste duplication across the five notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: Each task notebook evaluates a single scenario (one prompt to the target model); even counting all five sibling notebooks the benchmark has ~5 items, far below the threshold for reliable conclusions.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes multiple distinct dimensions: deterministic structural compliance (word count, lexical purity), and a multi-judge approval ratio across 4 separate judge models with a 75% threshold — distinct constraint-adherence vs. nuance dimensions.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task names/descriptions specify the conceit (explain modern tech to a historical figure under lexical constraints, testing conceptual mapping) but provide no research motivation or comparison to existing evaluations.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Inline comments label each stage (lexical guillotine, stone masons, the senate) and the prompt itself documents the protocol, but there is no dataset/schema documentation or markdown walkthrough of scoring edge cases.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a domain (cross-domain conceptual translation under constraints) but lacks experimental controls (no paired/control conditions), so failures cannot be cleanly attributed to the target capability vs. constraint-following or formatting compliance.

9_item_diversity.justification: Natural variation across notebooks (different eras, figures, languages: Ge'ez/English/Spanish, different forbidden-token sets and word windows), but no structured difficulty taxonomy or sub-condition labeling within the scoring code.

10_model_coverage.justification: The target model is a single kbench.llm per run (the four named keys are judges, not evaluated subjects); only one subject-model run is evidenced.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Hybrid scoring: deterministic word-count and forbidden-token (lexical guillotine) checks are unambiguous, but the substantive judgment uses a 4-judge LLM senate with criteria phrased as yes/no questions (3-4 dimensions). The rubric questions lack anchored examples/score scales, so the LLM-judge component is a vague-ish multi-dimension rubric.

2_construct_validity.justification: Each notebook genuinely calls the target model (raw=llm.prompt(prompt)), extracts the spoken block, then scores it. benchmarks.title: Adversarial Cognitive Profiling (ACP): Metacognitive Calibration Benchmark

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each World-Swap item has a deterministic correct vs naive answer; classification uses a multi-layer slop detector (keyword, numeric, embedding, token-overlap) plus a verbose-correct guard and abstention handling, all in code.

2_construct_validity.justification: Deliberately isolates metacognition via paired conditions (Tier-1 identity rules vs Tier-2+ swaps) and scaffold-equalized prompts that strip the structural confound, so performance deltas attribute to inhibitory/metacognitive control rather than formatting.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, seeded, parallelized with joblib, structured-output schema, protobuf sanitization, _safe_call exception wrapping and graceful fallbacks throughout.

4_sample_size.justification: Generates and evaluates 600 items (150 per tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types).

5_metric_design.justification: Reports many distinct dimensions — d', meta-d', M-ratio, ACE, selective-prediction, tool-utility, ECE, calibration bins, entropy — with bootstrap 95% CIs and justification in markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: The header markdown names the track, primary metric and four failure modes clearly, but offers limited positioning of the gap relative to existing metacognition benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Extensive sectioned markdown plus documented dataset generation, metric definitions, prompt design rationale and calibration protocol enable reproduction.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (metacognitive efficiency / inhibitory control) with explicit control-vs-perturbation tiers and an inverse-scaling hypothesis, reported per tier.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a 4-type x 4-tier taxonomy and the profile reports per-tier accuracy and confidence, revealing a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only a single saved run (claude-opus-4-7) is observable; no cross-model results are present in the materials.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Every World-Swap item has a deterministically generated correct_answer and naive_answer; correctness uses normalized containment plus a multi-layer slop pipeline (keyword, numeric proximity, embedding, token-overlap) with a verbose-correct guard. Ground truth is fully computable and unambiguous.

2_construct_validity.justification: Genuinely measures the model: _unified_infer calls llm.prompt(...) with a structured schema for all 600 items and scores actual responses. Construct is tightly isolated via scaffold-equalized Tier1 vs Tier2+ prompts (only world-rule context differs), paired naive/correct answers, and SDT meta-d' to separate metacognition from raw accuracy.

3_code_quality.justification: Modular, well-commented, SDK-idiomatic: dataclass items, seeded determinism, parallel inference with _safe_call exception capture, protobuf sanitization, structured-output schema, fallback code extraction, and bootstrap CI computation.

4_sample_size.justification: N=600 items (150 per tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types), far exceeding 20 and enabling per-tier/per-type and SDT analysis.

5_metric_design.justification: Many genuinely distinct dimensions: d'/meta-d'/M-ratio (SDT), ACE (asymmetric Brier), selective-prediction score, tool utility, ECE, confidence entropy, four failure-mode rates, inverse-scaling index, plus bootstrapped 95% CIs — with markdown justifying calibration and design.

6_problem_statement.justification: Header clearly names the capability (metacognition / SDT metacognitive efficiency), the four task constructs, four failure modes, the primary metric, and the design rationale (inverse scaling, world-swap to suppress priors) — a specific, well-motivated statement.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Sectioned markdown (World-Swap engine, slop detection, dataset generator, evaluation, prompts, aggregator), explicit confidence scale/bin definitions, scoring rules, and metric interpretation — fully reproducible.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (inhibitory control + calibrated metacognition under counterfactual rules) with explicit experimental controls: identity-rule Tier 1 baseline, scaffold-equalized prompts, paired naive vs correct, difficulty tiers, and a stated hypothesis (inverse-scaling/calibration).

9_item_diversity.justification: Items organized into a 4-type x 4-tier taxonomy with the notebook reporting per-tier accuracy/confidence, failure-mode breakdowns, and calibration bins — revealing where models fail, not just how often.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run artifact shows a single subject model (claude-opus-4-7) and one execution; no cross-model comparison is present in the captured material.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Every World-Swap problem has a deterministically generated correct and naive answer (apply_operator_swap, apply_ling, apply_logic, PHYSICS_RULES). Verification normalizes and uses multi-layer matching (verbose-correct guard, substring, numeric proximity, embedding/token-overlap) with structured output (UnifiedResp), making correctness fully verifiable from code.

Construct (metacognitive efficiency) is isolated via SDT meta-d'/d', and a deliberate confound control: TIER1_PROMPT is scaffold-equalized with UNIFIED_PROMPT (identical structure, differing only in world rules) to remove scaffolding as a confound for tier comparisons.

benchmarks.title: Can LLMs Inhibit Optimization in Supply-Chain Decision-Making?

5_metric_design.justification: A single performance dimension is computed (mean ΔNLL/step); no second distinct metric (e.g. cost, calibration) is reported and there is no markdown justification of the metric or its limitations.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Code-level section comments and docstrings describe the simulator, ΔNLL scoring, and prompt mechanics, but there are no markdown cells documenting dataset provenance (the hardcoded Sterman 11 sessions), metric interpretation, or a step-by-step walkthrough for reproduction.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single performance dimension is computed — mean ΔNLL per step (nanmean over sessions/roles); the Player class tracks revenue/costs but these are never reported, so effectively one metric with no second distinct dimension.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability (resisting the prepotent over-ordering/bullwhip response) with an explicit experimental control: baseline prompt vs. enhanced anti-overreaction guidelines prompt, allowing attribution of behavior change to the inhibition manipulation.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary by 11 distinct human-calibrated sessions and 4 roles (retailer/wholesaler/distributor/factory), giving natural variation, but ΔNLL is aggregated by a single nanmean with no structured difficulty tiers or per-role/per-session breakdown reported as the scored output.

10_model_coverage.justification: Both notebooks run only kbench.llm (a single harness model); no model name diversity appears in outputs, so the task was run on one model.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is well-defined as a continuous ΔNLL of the model's order series vs. human-calibrated and leave-one-human-out baselines (deterministic, reproducible). It is not exact-match correctness but a principled numeric score; parse_order_from_response uses a fragile regex that returns 0 on any unparseable line, which can silently corrupt scoring, keeping this at Level 1.

2_construct_validity.justification: The Beer Game genuinely operationalizes supply-chain ordering/over-reaction (the 'bullwhip' / inhibition-of-optimization claim), and task-2 adds a controlled prompt variant to isolate prompting effects. However the score depends heavily on prompt formatting and a brittle integer parser, and 'human-likeness via ΔNLL' conflates the claimed inhibitory-control construct with mimicry of noisy human heuristics, introducing moderate confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Code is functional, runs end-to-end, and uses kbench.task/kbench.chats.new per item for fresh context, but each notebook is a single monolithic code cell with no markdown, the order parser uses a coarse regex with a silent 0 fallback, and there is no handling of malformed model output beyond that.

4_sample_size.justification: NUM_SESSIONS=11 with 4 roles yields 44 ΔNLL evaluation units per run, and each session is a 24-round interactive episode, well above the >20-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: A single performance dimension is computed (mean ΔNLL/step, a likelihood-fit distance to human behavior). It is non-trivial and somewhat justified in the docstring, but only one underlying dimension is reported (no second distinct metric such as cost or calibration), so it sits at Level 1.

6_problem_statement.justification: The docstrings name the capability ('Baseline Executive Control', minimizing inventory cost / human-likeness) and frame the Beer Game, but there is no public task-page markdown and no articulation of the research gap relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The notebooks have clear section banners and a docstring explaining the ΔNLL scoring and Sterman baseline, and the dataset (11 calibrated sessions) is embedded inline. But there are no markdown cells, no schema description, and edge-case/scoring behavior (e.g., the 0-fallback parser) is undocumented.

8_capability_targeting.justification: The task probes a specific sub-capability (resisting over-ordering / matching human bounded-rational ordering under delays) with an explicit experimental control: task-1 (baseline prompt) vs task-2 (enhanced decision-guidelines prompt), enabling attribution of performance change to the prompt manipulation.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary across 11 distinct human-calibrated parameter sets and 4 structurally different roles (retailer/wholesaler/distributor/factory), giving natural variation, but there is no explicit difficulty taxonomy or per-condition breakdown reported beyond per-role ΔNLL printouts.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook runs a single kbench.llm instance; no loop over multiple distinct models and no multi-model comparison is present in the code or outputs.

benchmarks.title: The Time Traveler's Dilemma: Empathy, Oversight, & the Stop Sign Effect

5_metric_design.justification: Combines deterministic constraints + a multi-judge vote, but all dimensions reduce to a single pass/fail correctness axis; no second dimension (calibration/latency) and judge biases (position/length) are not mitigated.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task descriptions name the construct (epistemic exile / explaining modern concepts within historical lexicon) and the design intent, but offer little motivation for why this matters or what gap it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Prompts and scoring logic are self-documenting via inline comments ('Stone Masons', 'Iron Wall', 'Senate'), but there is no separate methodology/metric-interpretation documentation or dataset description.

2_construct_validity.justification: The task claims to measure conceptual mapping/epistemic-boundary empathy, and the lexical guillotine plus persona framing do target that, but a large share of pass/fail is driven by instruction-following (word count, bracket format, banned tokens) rather than the claimed empathy/conceptual-translation capability, introducing a moderate confound.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and readable with regex extraction and judge aggregation, but each notebook is a near-duplicate copy-paste of a single function, parsing is brittle (returns on first format failure), and there is no shared module or dataset abstraction.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook evaluates exactly ONE scenario (one prompt); across the 5 notebooks there are only 5 items total, far below 10, so a single response swings the score.

5_metric_design.justification: Combines deterministic structural metrics (word count, lexical purity) with a multi-judge nuance vote, but all roll up to pass/fail correctness on one dimension; no calibration/latency dimension and no documented mitigation of judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task names/descriptions clearly state the capability (explaining modern concepts within historical epistemic boundaries) and motivation, but do not connect it to a research gap relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Docstrings and inline section comments explain the pipeline, but there is no markdown task page, no schema documentation, and judge-selection/threshold rationale is only implicit.

2_construct_validity.justification: The 2x2-ish design (concept x native-language vs English baseline) does give some isolation, but the eval heavily conflates the claimed 'empathy/conceptual mapping over decay' capability with constraint-following (word count, banned-token avoidance) and target-language fluency; failures can plausibly stem from formatting/lexical compliance rather than the cognitive skill claimed.

3_code_quality.justification: Code is functional with sensible regex extraction and a senate-fail-safe (asserts when fewer than 4 judges resolve), but it is a single monolithic function per notebook with no modularity, no markdown, hardcoded judge keys, and a try/continue that silently drops judges that fail to return results.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook is a single hand-crafted scenario (one prompt) and the whole task spans only 5 notebooks/scenarios total — far below 10 items, so a single lucky/unlucky generation swings the score.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple checks are computed (word-count compliance, lexical leakage, and 4 judge dimensions), but they largely measure the same pass/fail compliance dimension and there is no second performance axis (e.g. calibration/latency) and no discussion or mitigation of LLM-judge biases (length/position).

6_problem_statement.justification: The @kbench.task descriptions name the capability (explaining modern concepts within an ancient epistemic frame, native-language vs English) but there is no task-page prose connecting it to a broader research question or gap; motivation is implicit in the scenario.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The notebooks contain zero markdown cells; the only documentation is the decorator description and inline docstring/comments. Dataset provenance, scoring rules, judge selection rationale, and metric interpretation are undocumented for a reader.

8_capability_targeting.justification: It targets a named domain (counterfactual/cross-temporal conceptual translation under lexical constraints) and even includes a control (English baseline vs native language), but the heavy constraint-compliance scoring means it does not cleanly isolate the conceptual-mapping mechanism from instruction-following.

9_item_diversity.justification: There is purposeful variation across the 5 notebooks (two concepts x two/three language conditions) giving some structure, but each scenario is bespoke with no shared difficulty tiers or sub-condition labels reported within a unified evaluation, so the gradient is informal.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each task runs the target eval on a single kbench.llm; the 4 'senate' models are judges, not models-under-test, so cross-model comparison of the target capability is not provided.

benchmarks.title: Embodied Inhibitory Control in AnimalAI

1_answer_correctness.justification: Answers are deterministically verifiable: vision uses exact integer match against goal counts derived from arena metadata, the text/embodied tasks score success against an unambiguous rule (GO only after DISTANCE==0 / total_reward > 1.1), with structured-schema parsing and a safe-default action parser.

2_construct_validity.justification: The suite uses paired control vs test conditions (control notebooks remove the prepotent yellow ramp reward / use absent goals) so performance differences isolate inhibitory control — withholding the prepotent GO toward the green goal until the rolling reward arrives — strongly aligning implementation with the claimed capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Idiomatic kaggle-benchmarks usage with @task + .evaluate() over datasets, per-trial chats.new() context, a typed Response schema, a dedicated GoNoGoKaggleWrapper that rejects invalid actions, try/finally env cleanup, and dynamic CSV/dir parsing robust to renames.

4_sample_size.justification: Coverage is mixed: the embodied test filters to only 5 arenas and the text test uses 5 scenarios (mitigated by 10 repeats each = 50 runs), while the vision test runs ~143 frames; the headline embodied evaluation is thin but the broader suite reaches the low-tens, landing at moderate adequacy.

5_metric_design.justification: Each task computes essentially one correctness dimension (fraction of arenas/scenarios passed, or exact-count accuracy); multiple complementary tasks exist but each reports a single accuracy-style metric with no second dimension (e.g. latency, calibration) or documented bias mitigation.

6_problem_statement.justification: The text notebook has a markdown header explaining the go/no-go inhibitory-control rules clearly, but the task-page-style motivation is thin (mostly section headers like '# Setup'/'# Run' in the embodied notebook) and does not articulate the research gap or why this discriminates models.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Partial: the text-task markdown documents the environment and pass/fail rules and the filename schema is parsed and commented, but dataset provenance (arena configs/frames), the control-vs-test filtering rationale, and metric interpretation are only partially documented across notebooks and lack a unified walkthrough.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (motor/inhibitory restraint of a prepotent GO response) with explicit experimental controls — control arenas without the competing reward and a difficulty gradient via ramp distance (2,4,6,8,10) — enabling attribution of failures to inhibition rather than perception.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a structured taxonomy via the ramp_N_main_X_ramp_Y_wall_Z naming, with a ramp-distance difficulty gradient and explicit test vs control filtering, and the vision task counts goals across varied object configurations — enabling a graded performance analysis.

10_model_coverage.justification: Outputs across the suite show two distinct models (google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview on the embodied and vision tasks, google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview on the text task), giving limited 2-model comparative coverage.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Answers are deterministically verifiable: vision uses exact integer match to a computed goal count, physics uses regex letter extraction against a known-correct shuffled MCQ key, and the go/no-go/embodied tasks have unambiguous pass/fail (GO only after DISTANCE==0, or total_reward>1.1) with safe parse fallbacks.

2_construct_validity.justification: Each task type ships paired control vs. test conditions (e.g. reward-absent control vs. reward-present test; positive-slope test trials) so performance differences isolate inhibitory control rather than perception or formatting; the physics MCQ even shuffles options per-trial to remove position bias, tightly matching the stated capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular code using actors.Actor, chats.new per trial, structured Response schemas, robust parsing with safe defaults, a custom GoNoGoKaggleWrapper that rejects invalid actions, try/finally env cleanup, dynamic CSV column discovery, and markdown documentation in several notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset has 297 config yml files and ~298 frame directories; vision/physics test runs over many filtered trials (vision ~143 trials per the 0.993 score) and text test runs 5 scenarios x 10 repeats = 50; aggregate item counts far exceed 20.

5_metric_design.justification: Each task reports only a single accuracy/pass-rate dimension (fraction passed, fraction correct, or mean reward>threshold); no second distinct dimension such as calibration, latency, or timing-error magnitude is computed despite the timing-rich setup.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook markdown clearly names the capability (embodied inhibitory control via an Animal-AI go/no-go task) and states the rules and pass/fail criteria, but does not articulate the broader research gap or how it differs from existing inhibition benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Several notebooks include markdown describing rules and conditions and the config naming scheme is self-documenting, but documentation is uneven (vision/physics notebooks are code-only) and there is no consolidated description of dataset provenance, the min_wait_times CSV derivation, or metric interpretation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (withholding a prepotent GO/approach response until a sliding reward settles) with explicit control vs. perturbation conditions across text, vision, physics, and full embodied modalities, enabling attribution of failures to inhibition rather than confounds.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a structured taxonomy via config names (ramp slope sign/magnitude, main/ramp/wall goal types) and split into labeled control vs. test subsets across four modalities; notebooks filter and report by these sub-conditions, enabling a performance gradient (e.g. vision 0.99 vs. embodied 0.8 vs. text-test 0.0).

10_model_coverage.justification: Across the eight notebooks two distinct models appear in outputs — google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview and google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview — giving limited 2-model coverage.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each sub-task has unambiguous, code-verifiable ground truth: go/no-go pass requires GO only when DISTANCE==0 (deterministic env check), vision uses exact integer match against _count_goals, physics uses exact letter match against a deterministically shuffled MCQ key, and the embodied task thresholds simulator reward (>1.1). Parsing has safe defaults.

Multiple distinct dimensions exist across the suite (inhibition pass-rate, perception accuracy, physics accuracy) and the physics task mitigates position bias by shuffling options, but within any task only one accuracy dimension is computed, and no calibration/latency or explicit bias discussion accompanies it.

6_problem_statement.justification: The text-task markdown clearly explains the go/no-go inhibitory-control rules and the AnimalAI framing, naming the capability. 2_construct_validity.justification: The task aims to measure heuristic/efficiency-paradox reasoning, but the prompt is ambiguous (an LLM can validly reason either way) so failures may reflect prompt ambiguity rather than the claimed capability; there is partial signal via the distance manipulation across notebooks.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and uses the judge API with a None-assessment guard, but it is minimal, hardcodes a single prompt per notebook, has no dataset/loop, and no description field on most tasks.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single pass/fail dimension (all judge criteria must pass) is computed, with no second metric and no mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

benchmarks.title: Constraint Adaptation Benchmark (CAB)

1_answer_correctness.justification: Scoring is fully deterministic and verifiable: code-golf/regex-golf run model output against pre-validated hidden asserts and measure exact character length; EGAT computes an exact fractional-improvement metric against a fixed embedding centroid, all in code.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementations are tightly aligned to the claimed 'constraint adaptation under feedback': models receive precise scoring rules and iterative feedback, and confounds are handled (whitespace-token filtering across tokenizer families, system-role-rejection fallback, reasoning-tag stripping) so performance reflects optimization-under-constraints.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, well-commented, modular code with robust parsing (_OPTION_RE, parse_and_validate), API-error retry with cooldown, consecutive-error abort, JSON trace dumps, and graceful handling of malformed/invalid responses.

4_sample_size.justification: Each sub-notebook has only 5 evaluation items (5 EGAT prompts, 5 code-golf tasks, 5 regex-golf sessions); even within a notebook this is below the 10-item threshold (the 10 'trials' are repeated attempts at the same item, not distinct items).

5_metric_design.justification: A single continuous performance dimension per notebook (fractional char/distance reduction, max over trials, mean over items) with a clear penalty for failure, but no second orthogonal dimension and no calibration/latency analysis.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown precisely states the capability (embedding-geometry awareness; syntactic compression; pattern compression), the exact scoring formula, configuration flags, and what high/low scores reveal.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Comprehensive: scoring tables, per-task baselines, config-flag tables, detailed inline rationale for design fixes (BPE boundaries, whitespace filtering, bypass tags), and dataset definitions inline — reproducible from the notebook.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific, well-defined sub-capabilities with explicit hypotheses and controls: EGAT varies L2-normalization and centroid target via flags; code/regex golf provide difficulty-graded baselines and iterative feedback to isolate compression-under-constraint ability.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly structured with labeled difficulty/baseline tiers (EGAT prompts of increasing token complexity 1->19 tokens; golf tasks/sessions with varying baseline lengths and titled categories) and the code reports per-item scores enabling a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Captured runs are single-model per notebook (kbench.llm); no cross-model comparison evidence in the outputs.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Verification is fully deterministic and objective: code-golf executes hidden unit-test assertions in a fresh namespace and measures exact char counts; regex-golf checks match/no-match lists; egat scores center-of-mass distance with order-preserving dedup, all unambiguous with explicit penalty handling for invalid outputs.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementations tightly match claims: 'constraint adaptation / syntactical compression' is measured by fractional char reduction against a validated baseline with passing tests required, and 'embedding geometry awareness' by actual CoM convergence, with careful tokenizer-fairness handling (whitespace filtering, prefix enforcement) to avoid cross-model confounds.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown clearly names the capability (code compression, regex compression, embedding-geometry navigation) and scoring, with motivation, but does not articulate the broader research gap relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong documentation: markdown scoring tables, configuration-flag tables, baseline-char tables, and detailed inline rationale for design choices (tokenizer fairness, dedup determinism, system-role handling), enough to reproduce.

benchmarks.title: Behavioral Metacognitive Control on Expert Questions in LLMs

1_answer_correctness.justification: Answers are verifiable in code via exact-letter match against correct_answer plus deterministic scoring tables; tasks use principled SDT computation (d-hat, type-2 AUC, MC ratio) and explicit reward functions tied to empirical difficulty, with robust JSON parsing and fallbacks.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation tightly isolates behavioral metacognition: Delegate Game contrasts ANSWER vs DELEGATE against empirical difficulty, Task 11 uses signal-present/signal-absent binary pairs for type-2 SDT, and Behavioral ER uses paired neutral/affective framing of the same question so shifts reflect affect, not content. Caveats (declared vs behavioral) are documented honestly.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular helpers (_safe_prompt with graceful error handling, backward-iterating JSON parser with keyword fallbacks, scipy-free norm_ppf), per-question CSV/JSON logging, parse-failure tracking, and partial-result handling on API errors. Code-only cells but well structured.

4_sample_size.justification: Each of the 11 tasks loads 80 GPQA Diamond items (Task 11 = 160 prompts); datasets contain 37-80 items per JSONL. Well above the >20 threshold (note: the captured single run aborted early on API error, but the task is designed for 80 items).

5_metric_design.justification: Computes multiple distinct dimensions: accuracy/reward, delegation rate, confidence calibration (entropy vs difficulty), and full type-2 SDT meta-sensitivity (d*/d-hat). Metric choices justified extensively in docstrings with references; handles indeterminate cases.

6_problem_statement.justification: Module docstrings articulate the exact capability (behavioral vs declarative metacognition), why it matters, the gap vs reporting confidence, links to specific experimental aims, and cite prior literature (Wang 2025, Sethi 2025, Maniscalco & Lau 2012).

7_technical_documentation.justification: Extremely detailed in-code docstrings describe dataset provenance, scoring rules, SDT methodology, and design notes, but there are no markdown cells walking through dataset schema/eval/metrics in the notebook proper; documentation lives entirely inside code strings.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (metacognitive efficiency / acting on uncertainty) with explicit hypotheses and experimental controls: difficulty-graded delegation rewards, signal/noise binary pairs, and paired affective vs neutral conditions enabling attribution to the target mechanism.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items span a structured battery of 11 distinct behavioral probes (delegate, declared probe, second-chance, entropy, teammate-delegate, ER/CI/EM/PI, DPP sequence, binary pairs) plus per-item empirical difficulty (0-1), enabling gradient analysis of where models fail.

10_model_coverage.justification: All captured run outputs are from a single model (openai/gpt-oss-120b); no second model present in any notebook's run results.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each task defines fully deterministic scoring against ground truth: delegate-game uses correct-answer letter + empirical difficulty bins, SDT task (t11) computes d-hat/d* from signal/noise trials, ER task uses confidence-shift + answer-flip composites. Robust backward-searching JSON parsing with letter/keyword fallbacks. Answers verifiable from code.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation tightly isolates behavioral metacognition via deliberate design: ANSWER-vs-DELEGATE rewards calibrated uncertainty (not just accuracy), t11 uses paired signal-present/signal-absent trials for type-2 SDT, ER compares the same question under neutral vs affective framing to isolate framing-driven instability. Docstrings explicitly distinguish behavioral from declarative metacognition.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular code with helper functions (_safe_prompt, _parse_json_response, _norm_ppf), graceful error handling, partial-result completion tracking, per-question CSV logging, and idiomatic @kbench.task decorators. Robust output parsing with multiple fallbacks. Lacks markdown cells but module-level docstrings are thorough.

4_sample_size.justification: Datasets contain 80 GPQA Diamond questions for the main tasks (t06/t07/t09 jsonl = 80; gpqa candidates = 80), well above the 20-item threshold; t11 generates 160 trials. (t08 dataset has 36 items.)

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple distinct dimensions across tasks: delegation reward + Delegation AUC-ROC, confidence-entropy calibration error vs empirical difficulty, full type-2 SDT (MC = d*/d-hat) with hit/FA rates. Calibration is a core metric alongside accuracy; metric choices justified in docstrings with references.

6_problem_statement.justification: Docstrings present a specific, well-motivated problem: isolating BEHAVIORAL metacognition (acting on uncertainty via delegation) vs declarative self-report, with explicit experimental aims, references (Sethi 2025, Wang 2025, Maniscalco & Lau 2012), and how each task fills a gap. (Evaluated from embedded notebook text since no separate task page provided.)

7_technical_documentation.justification: Extensive module-level docstrings document scoring rules, dataset provenance, costs, and design rationale, but notebooks contain zero markdown cells and lack per-column schema/step-by-step walkthrough. Documentation lives only in code comments, falling short of comprehensive cross-artifact docs.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (behavioral metacognitive monitoring) with explicit hypotheses and experimental controls: difficulty-graded delegation rewards, paired neutral/affective conditions, and signal/noise SDT pairs that attribute performance to meta-sensitivity rather than object-level accuracy.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items carry difficulty bins (diff_bin column 0.8-0.9 etc.) and span multiple tasks/conditions; the candidate CSV stratifies by empirical difficulty and category, and scoring uses difficulty thresholds, enabling gradient analysis of where models fail.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each of the 11 notebooks defines a deterministic, fully-coded scoring rubric (e.g. delegate-game maps choice/confidence/difficulty to fixed reward tiers; confidence-entropy computes |norm_entropy - empirical_difficulty|), with ground truth from GPQA correct answers and empirical difficulty. benchmarks.title: Russian Doll Bench: Infrastructure Building Capabilities

1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is verified fully deterministically: each terminal exposes an _online flag and the server /status endpoint reports online/total counts; a task only 'passes' when all nested terminals are brought ONLINE via correct cryptographic decode/auth (e.g. Sys32Terminal._handle_authenticate checks decoded token == self._token). No subjective judging; ground truth is computed in code.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure cross-run agentic learning and progress retention by presenting increasingly complex nested-terminal configurations; the implementation operationalizes exactly this via a persistent filesystem across runs, a difficulty ladder of generators (T1 -> composite T1-T5 chains), and scoring on generators-passed minus an efficiency fine, tightly isolating the claimed capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular package: VirtualFileSystem with sandbox escape checks, separate genai/openai tool loops, robust tool dispatch with arg validation and graceful exception handling, retries on /status, per-task checkpoint/restore, and @kbench.task decorators used idiomatically. The notebook itself is a thin entry point but the dataset source is well-structured.

4_sample_size.justification: TaskSpecGenerator yields tasks_count=50 instances per spec and run_adaptive_learning configures 10 generators of escalating difficulty; well over 20 evaluation items, with _find_easiest_unsolved_task iterating across all of them.

5_metric_design.justification: Two distinct dimensions are tracked: a success/progress score (100 * fully_passed_generators) and an efficiency penalty derived from total iterations/tokens spent; the system prompt explicitly documents that success rate and token/iteration efficiency are both measured. RunTelemetry also logs token and activation metrics.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook markdown and task description ('Assesses the model's capacity for cross-run learning and progress retention') and the system.md state the capability and goal clearly, but there is no explicit positioning against existing benchmarks or articulation of the gap filled.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The system prompt and per-terminal .md files document protocols and the nested-terminal mechanism, and notebook markdown explains setup steps, but there is no consolidated documentation of dataset schema, scoring rules, or a metric-interpretation walkthrough; the eval logic must be reverse-engineered from tasks.py/infrastructure.py.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (long-horizon agentic problem-solving with cross-run knowledge persistence) with an explicit difficulty gradient (control single terminal up to composite multi-type chains) acting as experimental controls, and a clear hypothesis that better agents solve harder configurations faster.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized into a structured difficulty taxonomy (10 generator specs from T1 to randomized T1-T5 composites) using 5 distinct terminal types (cipher, hash, maze, bitmixer, delta-base32); the harness reports which generator/task index the agent fails on, enabling a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Notebook outputs are stripped and no run artifacts evidence more than a single model; only one default kbench.llm execution path is shown, so cross-model coverage cannot be established (treated as 1 model).

1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is fully deterministic and verifiable in code: terminals expose a /status endpoint and the benchmark checks 'online'/'done' flags after running the model-authored main.py against seeded terminal configs. Ground truth (auth tokens, encodings) is generated from seeds and validated by exact match; extensive unit_tests.py per terminal confirm correctness.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure cross-run learning/progress retention via increasingly complex nested-terminal tasks; implementation operationalizes this with a persistent filesystem across runs, a difficulty-graded sequence of generators (T1 .. T1|T2|T3|T4|T5 chains), and a score that rewards passing harder generators while penalizing iterations spent (efficiency). Design tightly matches the stated capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Large, modular, well-documented codebase (envs, infrastructure, task_generator, telemetry, per-terminal modules with markdown specs and unit/trace tests). Uses kbench @task decorators, isolated VFS per run with sandbox-escape checks, retries, graceful exception handling, and dual GenAI/OpenAI tool loops. The thin notebook itself is sparse but the executed logic is high quality.

4_sample_size.justification: TaskSpecGenerator uses tasks_count=50 per generator and there are 10 generators with seed-derived variation, far exceeding 20 evaluation items; _find_easiest_unsolved_task iterates over the full set of seeded validating terminals.

5_metric_design.justification: Score combines two distinct dimensions: success (number of fully-passed difficulty generators) and efficiency (penalty proportional to total iterations spent), with telemetry also tracking token metrics and terminal-activation steps. The compound success-plus-efficiency score is justified in the system prompt and goes well beyond single accuracy.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook markdown only explains setup steps; the capability framing (mastering activation of unknown nested-terminal configs, learning across runs) appears in the system.md prompt rather than a clear task-page problem statement. Capability is nameable but motivation/gap relative to existing evals is not articulated for a reader.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Dataset source code is extensively documented: per-terminal .md specs (e.g., sys32 delta-base32 encoding), docstrings, trace_test/unit_tests, and detailed system prompt describing protocol and scoring. Combined notebook + dataset give a reproducible picture, though the notebook markdown alone is thin.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (token/iteration-efficient skill acquisition and retention across runs) with a built-in difficulty gradient acting as an experimental control: easy single-terminal tasks up to deep composite chains, with score deltas attributable to how far the agent progresses and how efficiently.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly structured into 10 difficulty-graded generators (single terminal, repeated, mixed-type, composite shuffled), and the validation loop reports which generator/task index the agent fails on (failed_on_generator/failed_on_task), enabling a clear performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Notebook runs a single injected kbench.llm and the captured execution errored on import (AttributeError: LocalEnvironment). No multi-model results are present in the notebook.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is fully deterministic and verifiable in code: the server tracks each terminal's online flag and _get_status returns score=status['online']/max=status['total']; a run is 'done' only when all nested terminals are brought online via correct decoding/auth (e.g. Sys32Terminal._handle_authenticate checks the exact decoded token). No subjective judging.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation tightly isolates the claimed capability (cross-run learning + agentic mastery of nested terminals). Difficulty is staged via composed terminal specs (T1, T1-T1, ... up to T1|T2|T3|T4|T5), a persistent filesystem enforces the cross-run learning claim, and scoring blends success count with token/iteration efficiency as stated. Failures map to inability to solve the puzzle, not formatting confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: The dataset source is clean, modular, and well-commented: separate codecs, terminals, server, telemetry, sandboxed VirtualFileSystem with path-escape checks, robust tool dispatch with bare-exception-free error handling, retries, and dual GenAI/OpenAI tool loops. Uses kbench.task decorator idiomatically. Minor: the public notebook itself errored on import (LocalEnvironment AttributeError) in the captured run.

4_sample_size.justification: TaskSpecGenerator yields tasks_count=50 instances per generator across 10 staged generators (validation finds the easiest unsolved task across hundreds of generated terminal configurations), far exceeding 20 evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes at least two distinct dimensions: success (online/total terminals) AND efficiency (total tokens and agentic iterations, with score = 100*passed_generators - fine where fine scales with iterations spent). benchmarks.title: Embodied Inhibitory Control in AnimalAI

1_answer_correctness.justification: All sub-tasks have unambiguous, code-verifiable ground truth: vision uses exact integer count match (_count_goals), physics uses exact MCQ letter match with a deterministic correct key, embodied/text use a deterministic reward/timing threshold (total_reward>1.1, GO only when DISTANCE==0). Parsing uses regex/structured Response schemas with safe defaults.

2_construct_validity.justification: Strong paired control-vs-test design isolates inhibitory control: the test condition requires withholding the prepotent GO until a reward rolls into the gutter, while control conditions (ramp absent / static scenes) verify the model can otherwise perceive/act. The text-only variant strips vision to confirm the failure is inhibition, not perception, and vision/physics sub-tasks separately validate prerequisite perception, directly controlling confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular use of the kbench SDK (@task, .evaluate over file/trial lists, chats.new fresh context per trial), structured Response dataclasses, robust action parsing (GoNoGoKaggleWrapper raises on invalid actions; _parse_letter / safe WAIT default), and physics shuffles MCQ options deterministically. Minor rough edge: the control notebook hit an unhandled 'response' variable error, and section markdown is thin in the env notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: Across the suite sample sizes are ample: vision loads ~143 frame trials (mode=='test', goals!=0) and physics ~45 positive-ramp MCQ trials. The embodied env test (5 configs) and text test (5 scenarios x 10 repeats = 50 runs) are individually small, but the benchmark as a whole far exceeds 20 evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Each task reports an accuracy/pass-rate, and the suite spans multiple complementary dimensions (perception count accuracy, physics-prediction accuracy, inhibition pass rate) plus explicit position-bias mitigation in physics (deterministic option shuffling per trial). However, within any single task only one accuracy-style metric is computed and there is little markdown justifying metric choice or discussing limitations, so it falls short of an explicitly justified multi-dimensional metric per task.

6_problem_statement.justification: The text-only notebook has a clear markdown problem statement (rules, what passing/failing means) and task docstrings name 'inhibitory control', but the env/vision/physics notebooks lack a task-page-level statement explaining why this capability matters or what gap relative to existing benchmarks it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Partial documentation: the text task has a Rules markdown section, config/frame naming encodes the condition schema, and min_wait_times.csv documents timing; but several notebooks (vision, physics) have no markdown describing dataset provenance, the filtering logic, or metric interpretation, so reproduction requires reading code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (prepotent-response inhibition in a go/no-go paradigm) with an explicit hypothesis and experimental controls: positive-ramp test conditions create the prepotent GO temptation, while control filters (ramp absent / flat) and the perception sub-tasks serve as baselines, allowing performance deltas to be attributed to inhibition rather than perception or acting.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized by a structured config taxonomy (ramp slope N, main/ramp/wall goal types) parsed into labeled metadata, and conditions are explicitly split into control vs test via regex filters; the text task stratifies by ramp distance (2/4/6/8/10) creating a difficulty gradient, enabling analysis of where models fail rather than just aggregate accuracy.

10_model_coverage.justification: Stored run metadata shows at least two distinct models across the suite (google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview on test notebooks and google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview on control/text notebooks), giving 2-3 models of comparative signal but not 4+.

1_answer_correctness.justification: All answers are deterministic and verifiable: go/no-go success is a precise rule (GO only once DISTANCE==0) checked via assert_true with failure reasons, and the vision task uses exact integer-count match against goal objects derived from config metadata.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation tightly isolates inhibitory control: the control condition removes the prepotent reward distractor while the test condition adds the moving yellow reward, so the only difference driving failures is the need to withhold a prepotent GO response, minimizing confounds via paired conditions.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular code using the SDK idiomatically (Actor subclasses, @task/.evaluate over scenarios, fresh chats.new per item), dataclass-structured responses, robust action parsing with a safe WAIT default, and markdown rule documentation in each notebook.

4_sample_size.justification: Well above 20: the text task runs 5 scenarios x 10 repeats = 50 evaluations, and the vision task evaluates ~143 trials (result 0.993 implies 142/143), with multiple modality variants.

5_metric_design.justification: Each task computes a single dimension (fraction passed / accuracy), but the suite includes a deliberate control-vs-test contrast that effectively yields a second comparative dimension; still no explicit calibration/latency metric or markdown justification of metric limitations within a single notebook.

6_problem_statement.justification: Markdown clearly names the capability (embodied inhibitory control via an Animal-AI go/no-go paradigm) and states the rules and rationale, but does not explicitly position it against existing LLM benchmarks or articulate the precise gap filled.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong documentation across notebooks: markdown 'Rules' sections describe the environment state, actions, pass/fail criteria; the dataset (configs, min_wait_times.csv, image frames) is concrete and load functions explain schema and frame-skip handling, enabling reproduction.
