10_model_coverage.justification: Run output shows a single model (deepseek-ai/deepseek-r1-0528); v2 ended in KeyboardInterrupt with no results, so no cross-model comparison is present.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Gold answers (agent_true_outcomes, deceptive_turns) are fully coded in gold_answer_json and scored with a multi-component deterministic formula: per-agent goal accuracy, set-based lie F1, confidence calibration term, plus invalid-outcome and lie-count penalties in v2, with robust JSON parsing and normalization helpers.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation operationalizes strategic theory-of-mind: from payoff tables plus deceptive dialogue the model must infer each agent's true goal and which turns were lies, directly matching the claimed deception-detection/intent-inference construct, with difficulty filtering (>=3) to target the discriminating range in v2.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular helpers (_normalize_turns, _normalize_goal_map, _parse_json_response with code-fence fallback, _f1, _safe_confidence) and idiomatic evaluate() over a DataFrame; v2 adds defensive try/except around gold parsing and clamping. Minus only that markdown is absent and the displayed run was interrupted.

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset holds 1200 scenarios (240 per difficulty 1-5); v1 evaluates all rows and v2 the 720 hard rows — far above 20 items.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes multiple distinct dimensions combined into the score: goal accuracy (correctness), deceptive-turn F1 (detection), and a confidence-calibration term (1-|conf-acc|), with v2 adding invalid-outcome and lie-count penalties — clearly more than one underlying dimension.

6_problem_statement.justification: There is no task-page narrative or markdown anywhere; both notebooks contain a single code cell with zero markdown, so a reader cannot learn the capability or motivation from a problem statement.

7_technical_documentation.justification: No markdown cells, no documented dataset provenance/schema, no metric-interpretation prose; the evaluation must be fully reverse-engineered from code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (inferring true goals and detecting deceptive turns under conflicting incentives) with a clear hypothesis, and includes a difficulty gradient (1-5, hard split filtered) acting as an experimental control for discriminating power.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary by labeled difficulty (1-5) and many distinct settings, and v2 filters on difficulty, but the evaluation code does NOT report results broken down by difficulty or setting, so the taxonomy is present in data but not exploited in reporting.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only deepseek-ai/deepseek-r1-0528 appears in outputs (v1 ran on it; v2 was interrupted before completion); single-model coverage.

benchmarks.title: TemporalBench: Validity Windows Beat Decay Functions in Temporal Reasoning

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth exists in the JSONL datasets and the temporal_ambiguity notebook cleanly separates correct vs naive answers, but verification is fragile (substring/token-overlap >0.7 matching) and the v1/v4 main eval compares the model's value against the full 'domain:subject:dXX:value' answer string, producing 0/734 correct in the shown run; correctness logic is inconsistent across notebooks.

2_construct_validity.justification: Construct validity is broken in part of the suite: the v2-pccc notebook's tasks (PastQueryTrap, ChangeDetection, CurrentQuery, CausalQuery) accept llm but never call the model at all -- they only assert the in-notebook store returns a non-None value, so they measure the harness's data store, not the model. Where the LLM is queried, scoring artifacts (0% scores, naive string matching) undermine attribution.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and includes a reusable temporal store, field-normalization, and event replay, but it is repetitive across notebooks, uses bare 'except Exception: pass' that silently swallows errors and inflates totals, has the answer-format mismatch bug, and disables most version/seed loops via commented-out lines.

4_sample_size.justification: Datasets are large (2000 questions per version-seed, 12 version/seed combinations; the temporal_ambiguity CSV has 2000 rows; v4 OverlapQuery alone ran 734 items), well beyond 20 items.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single accuracy metric is computed per task with no calibration, latency, or second dimension; the key_findings block (System A near/far accuracy, TRS, correlations) is hardcoded prose in the output JSON, not actually computed by the notebook.

6_problem_statement.justification: Markdown names the capability (temporal reasoning: validity windows vs decay) and the track, and the task families have descriptions, but motivation is thin and the 'validity windows beat decay' claim is asserted rather than developed or contextualized against existing temporal-reasoning benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Some notebooks have markdown headers, dataset paths, and the underlying benchmark/ source defines the data model, but scoring rules, the answer-format expectations, and metric interpretation are under-documented and the as-of/decay distinction is not clearly explained for reproduction; documentation is uneven across the 5 notebooks.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets temporal reasoning with a task-family taxonomy (AsOfQA, ChangeDetection, CausalQuery, PastQueryTrap, DecayTrap, OverlapQuery) and a validity-window-vs-decay hypothesis, but lacks clean controls in the LLM-facing eval and several 'tasks' don't probe the model, so attribution to the target capability is weak.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly labeled by task_family and by version/seed tiers (v1 baseline through v4 adversarial overlap, plus difficulty traps), and tasks are split per family so results can be reported per condition, giving a structured gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run artifacts/output references span three distinct models (google/gemini-2.5-flash, zai/glm-5, anthropic/claude-opus-4-7) across the notebooks, qualifying for the 2-3 model band.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth exists in the dataset (deterministic as-of answers), but verification is broken or fragile across notebooks: the eval notebook scores free-form LLM output against internal encoded IDs (e.g. 'slow:spec_15:d14:v5') via substring/token overlap, yielding 0/734; the v2/v4 notebooks never call the LLM and merely assert the Python store returns non-None (and one uses a nonexistent 'expected_value' field). Only task-2 has a sensible (but still substring-based, 'v2' in 'v26') check.

2_construct_validity.justification: Several 'tasks' fail to measure the model at all: temporalbenchv4 and temporalbench-v2-pccc pass llm as an unused parameter and instead test the authors' TemporalAttentionStore retrieval logic, so reported scores (942/942, 734/734) reflect the data structure, not the LLM. The eval notebook's encoded-ID answer format guarantees failures unrelated to temporal reasoning. Clear mismatch between claim and what is measured.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and is moderately organized with helper functions, but has serious defects: tasks that ignore the llm argument, computed-but-unused 'answer'/'expected' variables, bare 'except Exception: pass' swallowing all errors in the eval loop, hardcoded placeholder 'key_findings', and a staleness notebook built entirely on mock hardcoded data.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset is large (v1 has 2000 questions; per-task families number in the hundreds, e.g. 734 OverlapQuery, 942 CurrentQuery; task-2 evaluates 2000 rows), well above 20 items even where scoring is flawed.

5_metric_design.justification: Effectively a single accuracy/pass-rate metric per task with no calibration, latency, or secondary dimension; the richer 'System A/B', TRS, and correlation findings are hardcoded strings in the output JSON, not actually computed. Several metrics are meaningless because the LLM is bypassed.

6_problem_statement.justification: The eval notebook header names the capability (temporal reasoning: validity windows vs decay) and track and gives basic context, but does not substantiate the motivation or gap, and the strongest framing claims (decay vs validity windows) are not actually evaluated in code.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Dataset has a metadata.json listing all version/seed resources and there is brief notebook markdown and a data-structure description, but scoring rules, the answer encoding scheme, and the relationship between the store and the LLM evaluation are undocumented and inconsistent across the five notebooks.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a clear domain (point-in-time temporal fact retrieval, with adversarial PastQueryTrap/Reversion/Overlap families serving as intended controls), but the execution does not isolate the capability: the kept runs either bypass the model or fail on formatting, so failing/passing reveals little about model temporal reasoning.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a structured taxonomy of task families (AsOfQA, ChangeDetection, CausalQuery, PastQueryTrap, CurrentQuery, DecayTrap, OverlapQuery, Reversion) across versions v1-v4 and seeds 0-2 plus an adversarial set, and tasks are reported per family, providing strong potential for a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook runs a single injected kbench.llm (visible runs include gemini-2.5-flash, glm-5, claude-opus-4-7 but each in its own separate notebook with one model); no within-task cross-model comparison is performed.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth (answer/correct_answer/naive_answer) exists in the large dataset and the task-2 ambiguity notebook checks it via substring match, but verification is fragile (case/substring only) and seriously broken elsewhere: the main eval scored 0/734 on v4 OverlapQuery, and the v2/v4 notebooks assert only that an internal Python store returns non-None, never comparing to the question's true answer.

2_construct_validity.justification: Most notebooks do not measure LLM temporal reasoning at all: the v2/v4 tasks call store.get() on a hand-written TemporalAttentionStore and assert it returns a value while never using llm, so 'CurrentQuery: 942/942 correct' reflects the Python data structure, not the model. The staleness notebook uses a 3-item MockStore. There is a clear mismatch between stated purpose and what runs.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and has reasonable helpers (normalize_fact, replay_events, get_question_day), but bare 'except: pass' swallows all errors in the eval loops, the main notebook hardcodes fabricated 'key_findings', tasks accept llm yet ignore it, and one notebook depends on MockStore mocks. Functional but with significant rough edges and dead/misleading logic.

4_sample_size.justification: The dataset is large (2000 questions per version/seed, multiple versions v1-v4 x seeds), and the task-2 ambiguity notebook evaluates over all 2000 rows; well above 20 items.

5_metric_design.justification: A single accuracy/pass-fail metric per task with minimal justification; the store-based notebooks effectively compute only data-presence rate, and the fabricated multi-line 'key_findings' (TRS, correlations, p-values) are hardcoded constants not derived from any computation, not a real metric suite.

6_problem_statement.justification: The eval notebook header names the capability (temporal reasoning, validity windows vs decay) with basic motivation and a competition/track, but does not rigorously articulate the gap or research question beyond slogans, and the description is undercut by tasks that do not test it.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Some documentation exists (dataset path/structure described, task families listed, a few markdown headers and the data-loading print of question keys), but scoring rules, the meaning of facts/events schemas, and how ground truth is derived are underspecified, and several notebooks have no markdown at all.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the right domain (point-in-time / as-of temporal QA with traps for stale-vs-current and overlapping validity windows) and defines task families as a coarse difficulty/condition split, but lacks working experimental controls because the harness largely fails to attribute performance to the model (no LLM in most tasks; broken scoring).

9_item_diversity.justification: Items carry task_family labels (AsOfQA, ChangeDetection, CausalQuery, PastQueryTrap, OverlapQuery, DecayTrap, CurrentQuery) and version tiers v1-v4 giving structured variation, but the notebooks do not report a clean per-condition gradient of model performance (results are 0/734 or measure the store), so the diversity is not turned into a usable performance breakdown.

10_model_coverage.justification: Across the collection different notebooks reference different models (google/gemini-2.5-flash, zai/glm-5, anthropic claude-opus in the run artifacts), giving 2-3 distinct models, though no single task systematically compares them.

benchmarks.title: The Efficiency Paradox: Measuring Heuristic Capture in Vehicle-Centric Planning

1_answer_correctness.justification: Uses an LLM-as-judge with explicit pass/fail criteria (4-5 per notebook), but the 'correct' answer is contestable: the intended answer (drive/move the car) conflicts with a genuinely valid alternative the models give (walk and carry a gas can), so the rubric encodes a single arguable solution without acceptance criteria for valid alternatives.

2_construct_validity.justification: Aims to measure heuristic/efficiency reasoning, but failures may reflect the judge penalizing the reasonable gas-can solution rather than a genuine reasoning deficit; the construct conflates the target with adherence to the author's preferred answer.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and uses assess_response_with_judge plus a None-guard for judge failure, but minimal: a single hardcoded prompt per notebook, no @task description, no dataset, and no output handling beyond the judge.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook evaluates exactly one hand-crafted prompt; even taken together the three notebooks total 3 items, far below the 10-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Single pass/fail dimension per item via judge criteria; no second performance dimension, no calibration, and no discussion or mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: The task title ('The Efficiency Paradox: Measuring Heuristic...') and prompts convey the intended capability, but neither notebook sets a @task description and there is no motivation, gap analysis, or explanation of why this probe matters.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebooks are code-only with no markdown, no description, no dataset documentation, and no explanation of the judging methodology or criteria rationale.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a named idea (heuristic override / efficiency reasoning) and the three distance variants (5m/50m/5km) form a rough gradient, but there are no real experimental controls and the single-prompt design cannot reliably attribute behavior to the claimed mechanism.

9_item_diversity.justification: The only structured variation is the distance manipulation across three separate notebooks (5m, 50m, 5km), a mild gradient, but within each notebook there is exactly one homogeneous item and no labeled taxonomy or in-notebook breakdown.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run results are None with no model identifiable; no evidence of more than one model being evaluated.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Uses an LLM judge with 3-5 binary pass/fail criteria, but the criteria are vague and lack anchored examples or score scales, and the premise's 'correct' answer (must drive, never walk with a gas can) is contestable — models give reasonable walking answers that the rubric marks as failures. Verification exists but is fragile and arguably mis-specified.

2_construct_validity.justification: Aims to measure heuristic/over-literal reasoning ('efficiency paradox'), but the prompts conflate this with a debatable factual premise; a model recommending walking with a gas can is reasonable, so failures may reflect disagreement with a flawed gold premise rather than the targeted reasoning deficit, introducing a confound.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and minimal: a single hardcoded prompt per notebook, run() once, and an LLM-judge call with a None-guard. No data loading, no normalization beyond the judge, no iteration; hardcoded values throughout and no modular structure.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook evaluates exactly one hardcoded prompt (1 item); even across all three notebooks (5m, 50m, 5km) there are only 3 distinct items total, far below the 10-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single pass/fail (all judge criteria must pass) is produced, with no secondary dimension, no justification in markdown, and no mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: The folder title and task names convey the intended capability (heuristic/over-literal reasoning in a refueling dilemma) and there is implicit motivation, but no clear written problem statement, motivation, or gap analysis is present in the notebooks (no markdown).

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebooks contain only code cells with no markdown, no description of the judge methodology, scoring rules, or dataset; a reader must reverse-engineer the intent and the contestable gold answer entirely from code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a single named idea (resisting an over-literal/heuristic walk recommendation) and the distance gradient (5m/50m/5km) is a mild control, but with one item per condition and a debatable gold answer there are no real experimental controls isolating the capability.

9_item_diversity.justification: The three notebooks vary one parameter (distance: 5m/50m/5km), giving surface-level variation, but each is a single homogeneous item with no structured taxonomy, no per-condition aggregation, and no difficulty stratification beyond distance.

10_model_coverage.justification: No model name appears in the truncated run outputs and each task is a single run() call; no cross-model comparison is present (at most one model).

1_answer_correctness.justification: Uses an LLM-judge with 3-5 binary criteria per notebook (assess_response_with_judge), which provides a rubric but each criterion is a single pass/fail with no anchored 0-N scale; moreover the intended 'correct' answer (must recommend driving) is debatable, so verification is fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: There is a clear mismatch: the task claims to measure heuristic/efficiency reasoning, but the 'gold' stance that walking is wrong is itself questionable (bringing a fuel can back is a valid real-world answer), so a 'failure' often reflects sound reasoning rather than the targeted deficit.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and short with an LLM-judge fallback (assert_fail if assessment is None), but each notebook hardcodes a single prompt string, uses .run() on one item, and has no dataset, no parsing of structured output, and no input handling.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook evaluates exactly ONE hardcoded prompt; even across the three notebooks there are only 3 items total, far below the 10-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single pass/fail outcome (all judge criteria must pass) is produced per notebook; no second dimension, no calibration, and no discussion or mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: The notebooks contain no markdown and no problem statement; only a bare task name and an inline prompt, so a reader cannot learn what capability is targeted or why from the artifacts provided.

7_technical_documentation.justification: No markdown cells, no dataset/provenance, no methodology or metric documentation; only code cells with a hardcoded prompt and judge criteria.

8_capability_targeting.justification: There is a loose unifying theme (resisting a 'walk' heuristic when the goal is refuelling the vehicle) varied across distances (5m/50m/5km), giving a rough gradient, but no experimental controls and the design does not cleanly isolate the claimed heuristic-override capability.

9_item_diversity.justification: The three notebooks vary one parameter (distance: 5m, 50m, 5km), which is natural surface variation across separate single-item tasks, but there is no structured taxonomy or per-condition reporting within a task.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook calls .run(kbench.llm) on a single model; no cross-model comparison present.

benchmarks.title: Constraint Adaptation Benchmark (CAB)

1_answer_correctness.justification: Fully deterministic verification: code-golf and regex-golf execute model submissions against hidden assert-based unit tests / MATCH-DO_NOT_MATCH lists in a fresh namespace, and EGAT scores against a third-party embedding geometry; correctness is exact (pass/fail per option) with character/length counts, no subjectivity.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure constraint adaptation / compression under feedback; the implementation operationalizes this precisely via multi-trial reprompting where the model must keep shortening a provably-correct solution, with the fractional-reduction metric directly reflecting the targeted skill and validity enforced by passing all hidden tests.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, well-commented: regex option extraction, sandboxed exec with traceback trimming, structured feedback builder, robust one-time system-prompt-rejection detection with baked-prompt fallback, fresh chat context per task, and idiomatic kbench.task/.run usage.

4_sample_size.justification: Each individual task notebook evaluates 5 sessions/tasks (below 20), but the CAB folder comprises three notebooks (code-golf, regex-golf, EGAT) totaling 15 distinct evaluation sessions, landing in the 10-20 band for the benchmark as a whole.

5_metric_design.justification: The fractional-reduction / fractional-improvement metric is appropriate and well-justified in markdown, but each task computes essentially a single aggregate dimension (mean reduction) with a -1.0 penalty; no second orthogonal dimension (e.g. correctness rate vs compression, or trials-to-solve) is reported.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook clearly states what it evaluates (syntactical/algorithmic compression, pattern compression, embedding-geometry navigation) and the scoring, but motivation is brief and there is no explicit positioning against existing code/regex benchmarks or articulation of the gap filled.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong documentation: each notebook opens with markdown describing the capability, the exact scoring formula, configuration flags, and a per-task baseline table; the dataset (tasks, baselines, hidden tests) is fully inlined and the evaluation protocol and feedback loop are explained in comments.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific, well-defined sub-capability (iterative constraint-driven compression under verifier feedback) with a clear hypothesis (better models achieve larger reductions while staying valid) and structured difficulty across tasks of increasing baseline length / constraint complexity, plus EGAT's configurable geometry controls.

9_item_diversity.justification: Tasks are explicitly organized into labeled tiers/sessions of escalating baseline size and constraint type (e.g. anchored class up to multi-constraint composites; tribonacci up to bracket/tag validators), and per-task scores are reported, enabling a performance gradient across difficulty.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only a single model (gemini, seen in one notebook output) is evidenced; notebook outputs are otherwise stripped and there is no artifact showing the suite run against multiple distinct models.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Verification is fully deterministic: code-golf runs the model's submitted code plus pre-validated hidden assert tests in a fresh namespace; regex-golf checks the regex fully matches the MATCH list and rejects the DO_NOT_MATCH list; EGAT computes exact embedding-geometry distances. Pass/fail and the continuous score are computed from objective execution, not judgment.

2_construct_validity.justification: Each task isolates a well-defined capability (constraint-satisfying code/regex compression; navigating a third-party embedding space) and the metric (fractional reduction toward a verified baseline) directly reflects it. Hidden tests prevent gaming, and the multi-trial reprompt loop with feedback isolates optimization-under-constraint rather than one-shot luck.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, heavily commented notebooks using kbench @task, regex-based option extraction, sandboxed exec with truncated tracebacks, a 10-trial feedback loop, and notably robust handling of system-prompt rejection (detect-once, restart task with baked-in prompt). EGAT wraps judge-model loading and parsing/validation carefully.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook contains only 5 evaluation items (5 golf tasks / 5 regex sessions / 5 EGAT prompts), below the 10-item threshold; the multi-trial reprompting increases attempts per item but not the number of distinct evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Uses a continuous, well-justified objective metric (fractional character/distance reduction vs a verified baseline, with -1.0 penalty for no valid solution, aggregated as mean across tasks) rather than plain accuracy; combines a validity dimension (passes hidden tests) with an efficiency/compression dimension, and the scoring rationale is documented in markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown names the capability and scoring clearly (e.g., 'evaluate syntactical efficiency and algorithmic compression', 'pattern compression and constraint reasoning'), but does not connect to a broader research question or articulate the gap relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong notebook documentation: markdown overview tables of all tasks with baseline sizes, explicit scoring formulas, configuration-flag tables (EGAT), and inline comments explaining the reprompt loop, baseline pre-validation, and edge-case handling, sufficient to reproduce the evaluation.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific sub-capabilities (constraint-bounded compression / embedding-space navigation) with a clear hypothesis (shorter-yet-valid solutions indicate stronger constraint reasoning) and a difficulty gradient across the 5 tasks of increasing baseline size/constraint complexity, allowing graded performance attribution.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized with labeled, increasing-difficulty tasks/sessions (baseline char counts 449..1465 for code, regex lengths 21..56 with named constraint types), and per-task scores are reported separately, yielding a clear performance gradient across the structured set.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each task is run against a single injected kbench.llm; the visible outputs show one model per notebook (EGAT's all-MiniLM is the judge embedding model, not an evaluated LLM), with no cross-model comparison.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Fully deterministic verification: Code Golf execs each submitted option against pre-validated hidden assert tests in a fresh namespace (pass/fail + exact char count); Regex Golf compiles patterns and checks re.fullmatch over MATCH/DO_NOT_MATCH lists; EGAT computes an exact geometric center-of-mass distance to a fixed centroid. No subjective judging.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation closely matches the 'constraint adaptation' claim: each sub-benchmark forces the model to optimize under an unusual hard constraint (shortest correct code, shortest valid regex, move token CoM toward a centroid) with a paired baseline so the score is a fractional improvement over a fixed reference, isolating adaptation/optimization rather than raw knowledge.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, well-commented, modular: regex-based option extraction, sandboxed exec with limited traceback, multi-trial reprompting with feedback, graceful API-error retry/backoff, system-role-rejection fallback to baked prompts, deterministic dedup ordering, and defensive re-imports. Idiomatic kbench.task usage with assertions.

4_sample_size.justification: Each sub-benchmark uses 5 tasks/prompts/sessions (5 code-golf tasks, 5 regex sessions, 5 EGAT prompts). Each is run over 10 trials, but the number of distinct evaluation items per task is 5 (10-20 range only if trials are counted), placing it at the lower-mid sample tier.

5_metric_design.justification: Beyond pass/fail, the metric is a continuous fractional-improvement score against a fixed baseline (char reduction, or CoM distance reduction D_initial->D_final), aggregated as a mean with per-item reporting and best-of-trials tracking; the markdown justifies the scoring and its sign conventions, and EGAT documents tokenizer/normalization choices to avoid model-family bias.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook's markdown clearly names its capability (embedding-geometry awareness, code compression, regex/pattern compression) and the scoring rationale, but does not tie them to a broader research question or explain the gap relative to existing benchmarks; motivation is present but not compelling/specific about novelty.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong notebook documentation: markdown explains scoring formulas, configuration flags, and per-task tables (baseline chars/length), and code comments thoroughly describe design choices (e.g. whitespace-token filtering rationale, set()-dedup determinism, system-role fallback). Schemas of the in-notebook datasets are explicit.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific, well-defined sub-capabilities (optimization under a precise hard constraint) with a clear hypothesis and built-in controls: a fixed validated baseline per item, difficulty tiers (e.g. EGAT prompt_001 single token up to prompt_005 19 adversarial tokens; regex baselines 21->56 chars), and best-across-trials tracking that exposes an adaptation gradient.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly structured into labeled difficulty tiers with per-item baselines and the code reports per-item scores (per-prompt/per-task lists) alongside the aggregate, enabling analysis of where models succeed (e.g. EGAT scores 0.33 on easy single-token vs near-0 on adversarial mixed-token prompts).

10_model_coverage.justification: Each sub-benchmark runs against a single target model via kbench.llm; the captured run shows one model and no cross-model comparison is built in.
